name: Area of Circle
description: Calculates the total area enclosed by a circle given its radius.
example: A circle with radius 5 cm has area A = π × 5² = 25π ≈ 78.54 cm²

name: Area of Triangle
description: Calculates the area of a triangle as half the product of its base and corresponding height.
example: A triangle with base 8 m and height 5 m has area A = 0.5 × 8 × 5 = 20 m²

name: Area of Rectangle
description: Calculates the area of a rectangle as the product of its length and width.
example: A rectangle 6 m long and 4 m wide has area A = 6 × 4 = 24 m²

name: Area of Trapezoid
description: Calculates the area of a trapezoid (trapezium) using the average of the two parallel sides times the height.
example: A trapezoid with parallel sides 6 and 10, height 4: A = 0.5 × (6+10) × 4 = 32 units²

name: Circumference of Circle
description: Calculates the perimeter (circumference) of a circle. Also expressible as C = πd where d is the diameter.
example: A circle with radius 7 has circumference C = 2π × 7 = 14π ≈ 43.98 units

name: Surface Area of Sphere
description: Calculates the total curved surface area of a sphere.
example: A sphere of radius 3 m has surface area SA = 4π × 3² = 36π ≈ 113.1 m²

name: Volume of Sphere
description: Calculates the volume of a sphere given its radius.
example: A sphere of radius 3 m has volume V = (4/3)π × 27 = 36π ≈ 113.1 m³

name: Volume of Cylinder
description: Calculates the volume of a right circular cylinder as the area of the circular base times the height.
example: A cylinder with radius 2 and height 10: V = π × 4 × 10 = 40π ≈ 125.7 units³

name: Volume of Cone
description: Calculates the volume of a right circular cone. Equal to one-third the volume of a cylinder with the same base and height.
example: A cone with radius 3 and height 9: V = (1/3) × π × 9 × 9 = 27π ≈ 84.8 units³

name: Pythagorean Theorem
description: In a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
example: A right triangle with legs 3 and 4: c = √(9+16) = √25 = 5

name: Quadratic Formula
description: Gives the roots of any quadratic equation ax² + bx + c = 0. The discriminant (b²-4ac) determines the nature of roots.

name: Binomial Theorem
formula: (x + y)ⁿ = Σ C(n,k) xⁿ⁻ᵏ yᵏ  (k=0 to n)
description: Expands powers of a binomial expression into a polynomial sum using binomial coefficients.
example: (x+y)³ = x³ + 3x²y + 3xy² + y³

name: Sum of Arithmetic Series
description: Calculates the sum of a finite arithmetic sequence where each term differs from the previous by a constant amount d.

name: Sum of Geometric Series
description: Calculates the sum of a finite geometric sequence. For |r|<1, the infinite sum converges to a/(1-r).

name: Distance Formula
description: Calculates the Euclidean distance between two points in a 2D plane, derived from the Pythagorean theorem.

name: Slope Formula
description: Calculates the slope of a line passing through two points, representing the rate of change of y with respect to x.

name: Midpoint Formula
description: Finds the point exactly halfway between two given points in a coordinate plane.

name: Exponential Growth and Decay
formula: y = a · eᵏᵗ  (growth: k>0, decay: k<0)
description: Models quantities that change proportionally to their current value, such as population growth, radioactive decay, or compound interest continuously compounded.
example: Population starting at 1000 with 3% continuous growth after 10 years: y = 1000 × e^(0.03×10) ≈ 1349.9

name: Mean (Arithmetic Average)
description: The arithmetic average of a set of values, calculated by summing all values and dividing by the count.

name: Variance
formula: σ² = Σ(xᵢ - μ)² / N  (population)  |  s² = Σ(xᵢ - x̄)² / (n-1)  (sample)
description: Measures the average squared deviation from the mean, quantifying how spread out the data values are.

name: Standard Deviation
description: The square root of variance. Measures dispersion in the same units as the original data. A low value indicates data points close to the mean.
example: If variance is 4, standard deviation σ = √4 = 2

name: Z-Score (Standard Score)
description: Measures how many standard deviations an element is from the mean. Used to standardize values from different distributions for comparison.
example: If mean=70, σ=10, a score of 85: z = (85-70)/10 = 1.5 standard deviations above mean

name: Pearson Correlation Coefficient
formula: r = Σ((xᵢ - x̄)(yᵢ - ȳ)) / √(Σ(xᵢ - x̄)² · Σ(yᵢ - ȳ)²)
description: Measures the linear correlation between two variables. r=1 is perfect positive correlation, r=-1 is perfect negative, r=0 indicates no linear correlation.
example: Height vs weight data showing r=0.85 indicates a strong positive linear relationship.

name: Linear Regression Slope
formula: b₁ = Σ((xᵢ - x̄)(yᵢ - ȳ)) / Σ(xᵢ - x̄)²
description: The slope coefficient in simple linear regression y = b₀ + b₁x. Represents the change in y for each unit increase in x.
example: If b₁ = 2.5, each 1-unit increase in x predicts a 2.5-unit increase in y.

name: Linear Regression Intercept
description: The intercept in simple linear regression, representing the predicted y value when x equals zero.

name: Combinations (nCr)
description: Counts the number of ways to choose r items from n items without regard to order.
example: C(5,2) = 5!/(2!×3!) = 120/12 = 10 ways to choose 2 items from 5

name: Permutations (nPr)
description: Counts the number of ways to arrange r items from n items where order matters.
example: P(5,2) = 5!/3! = 120/6 = 20 ordered arrangements of 2 from 5

name: Bayes' Theorem

variables.P(A|B): posterior probability of A given B

description: Describes how to update the probability of a hypothesis given evidence. Fundamental to Bayesian statistics and machine learning.
example: Medical test: P(Disease|Positive) = P(Positive|Disease)×P(Disease) / P(Positive)

name: Normal Distribution PDF
description: The probability density function of the Gaussian (normal) distribution, the most important continuous probability distribution in statistics.
example: Standard normal (μ=0, σ=1): f(0) = 1/√(2π) ≈ 0.3989

name: Sine Definition
description: The sine of an angle in a right triangle is the ratio of the length of the opposite side to the hypotenuse. Extends to the unit circle for all angles.
example: In a 3-4-5 triangle, sin(θ) for the angle opposite the side of length 3: sin(θ) = 3/5 = 0.6

name: Cosine Definition
description: The cosine of an angle in a right triangle is the ratio of the adjacent side to the hypotenuse.
example: In a 3-4-5 triangle: cos(θ) for the angle between the hypotenuse and side 4: cos(θ) = 4/5 = 0.8

name: Tangent Definition
description: The tangent is the ratio of the opposite to adjacent side, or equivalently sin divided by cos. Represents the slope of the line at angle θ.
example: In a 3-4-5 triangle: tan(θ) opposite side 3: tan(θ) = 3/4 = 0.75

name: Law of Sines

variables.a,b,c: side lengths opposite to angles A, B, C

description: Relates the lengths of the sides of a triangle to the sines of its angles. Useful for solving non-right triangles (AAS, ASA, SSA cases).

name: Law of Cosines
description: Generalizes the Pythagorean theorem to non-right triangles. Used when two sides and the included angle (SAS) or all three sides (SSS) are known.

name: Pythagorean Identity
description: The fundamental trigonometric identity, derived from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the unit circle. Used to simplify trig expressions.
example: If sin(θ) = 0.6, then cos²(θ) = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64, so cos(θ) = 0.8

name: Double Angle Formula for Sine
description: Expresses the sine of a double angle in terms of the sine and cosine of the original angle.

name: Double Angle Formula for Cosine
description: Expresses the cosine of a double angle. Has three equivalent forms depending on which is most useful for simplification.

name: Euler's Formula
description: Establishes the deep relationship between the complex exponential function and trigonometry. Setting θ=π gives Euler's identity: e^(iπ) + 1 = 0.
example: e^(iπ) = cos(π) + i×sin(π) = -1 + 0 = -1, yielding the famous identity e^(iπ) + 1 = 0

name: Derivative Power Rule
description: The most basic differentiation rule. The derivative of x to the power n is n times x to the power (n-1).
example: d/dx [x⁵] = 5x⁴  |  d/dx [x²] = 2x  |  d/dx [x] = 1

name: Product Rule
description: Rule for differentiating the product of two functions: derivative of first times second plus first times derivative of second.
example: d/dx [x² sin(x)] = 2x sin(x) + x² cos(x)

name: Quotient Rule
description: Rule for differentiating a ratio of two functions. Mnemonic: 'low d-high minus high d-low over low-squared'.
example: d/dx [sin(x)/x] = (cos(x)·x - sin(x)·1) / x² = (x cos(x) - sin(x)) / x²

name: Chain Rule
description: Rule for differentiating composite functions. The derivative of a composition is the derivative of the outer function evaluated at the inner, times the derivative of the inner.
example: d/dx [sin(x²)] = cos(x²) · 2x

name: Integral Power Rule
description: The reverse of the power rule for differentiation. Raises the exponent by 1 and divides by the new exponent. The constant C accounts for all antiderivatives.
example: ∫ x³ dx = x⁴/4 + C  |  ∫ x dx = x²/2 + C

name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
formula: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = F(b) - F(a)  where F'(x) = f(x)
description: Links differentiation and integration. The definite integral of f from a to b equals the antiderivative evaluated at b minus at a.

name: Area Under Curve
description: The definite integral of f(x) between a and b gives the signed area between the curve and the x-axis. Use |f(x)| to always get positive area.
example: Area under y=x² from 0 to 3: ∫₀³ x² dx = [x³/3]₀³ = 9

name: Taylor Series

variables.f⁽ⁿ⁾(a): nth derivative of f evaluated at a

description: Represents a smooth function as an infinite sum of polynomial terms derived from its derivatives at a single point a. When a=0, called Maclaurin series.

name: Greatest Common Divisor (Euclidean Algorithm)
formula: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b)  until b = 0
description: Efficiently computes the largest integer that divides both a and b without remainder by repeatedly applying the modulo operation.

name: Prime Counting Approximation
description: The Prime Number Theorem states that the number of primes up to n is approximately n divided by the natural log of n. The approximation improves for larger n.

name: Fermat's Little Theorem
formula: aᵖ ≡ a (mod p)  ⟺  aᵖ⁻¹ ≡ 1 (mod p)  (p prime, gcd(a,p)=1)
description: If p is prime and a is not divisible by p, then a raised to the power p-1 is congruent to 1 modulo p. Used in primality testing and RSA cryptography.

name: Modular Arithmetic
description: Two integers are congruent modulo m if they have the same remainder when divided by m. Arithmetic operations are preserved under congruence.
example: 17 ≡ 2 (mod 5) since 17-2=15 is divisible by 5. In clock arithmetic: 14:00 ≡ 2 (mod 12)
